title: The Gang Carries a Corpse Up a Mountain

jokes.text: You got piss in that canteen? No, I got Gatorade in that canteen. The other one's got the piss in case things go bad.
jokes.setup: Charlie asks about Frank's overpacked supplies for a day hike
jokes.punchline: Frank casually reveals one canteen has piss as a backup plan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank moment - absurd preparation meeting disgusting practicality. The casual delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You got piss in that canteen?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: No, I got Gatorade in that canteen. The other one's got the piss in case things go bad.

jokes.text: I see you all fell for my ruse. What ruse? You asking us to go on a hike with your dad, and us saying yes? Oldest trick in the book.
jokes.setup: Charlie claims he tricked them into coming
jokes.punchline: The 'trick' is just asking someone to do something and them agreeing
jokes.explanation: Charlie's warped logic about basic social interaction being a master manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I see you all fell for my ruse.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What ruse?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You asking us to go on a hike with your dad, and us saying yes? Oldest trick in the book.

jokes.text: You know, no offense, Charlie, but Shelley's fat as shit. Oh, no offense taken, Dennis, because he is fat as shit, but, um, he won't really mind us saying that because he's dead.
jokes.setup: Dennis apologizes for calling Charlie's dad fat
jokes.punchline: Charlie casually reveals his dad is dead so the insult doesn't matter
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark escalation with Charlie's matter-of-fact delivery making the reveal even more shocking

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: You know, no offense, Charlie, but Shelley's fat as shit.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh, no offense taken, Dennis, because he is fat as shit, but, um, he won't really mind us saying that because he's dead.

jokes.text: What? What the hell are you talking about? I'm talking about Shelley Kelly, my dad. He's dead. Oh. Shit.
jokes.setup: The group processes Charlie's casual death announcement
jokes.punchline: The underwhelming 'Oh. Shit.' response after a long pause
jokes.explanation: The awkward timing and understated reaction to major news

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What? What the hell are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm talking about Shelley Kelly, my dad. He's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh. Shit.

jokes.text: All right? Look, it's a Kelly family burial tradition. We're just gonna carry the man to the top of the mountain, and then we're gonna chuck him over the side.
jokes.setup: Charlie explains his family's burial traditions
jokes.punchline: The casual use of 'chuck him over the side' for what should be a solemn ritual
jokes.explanation: Dark humor in treating corpse disposal as casual tradition, enhanced by Charlie's earnest delivery

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: All right? Look, it's a Kelly family burial tradition. We're just gonna carry the man to the top of the mountain, and then we're gonna chuck him over the side.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, why would I want to ruin my essence, Mac? I'm not doing it.
jokes.setup: Mac mentions Dennis's Vitruvian man back symmetry
jokes.punchline: Dennis referring to his physical form as his 'essence' that could be ruined
jokes.explanation: Pure Dennis vanity - treating his physical appearance as something mystical and precious

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, why would I want to ruin my essence, Mac? I'm not doing it.

jokes.text: So I'm just gonna blurt it right out. Bros before hoes. Uh, he's right. Bros before hoes. Yeah, he's got us. Oh, my God. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Charlie needs to convince them to help with his dead father
jokes.punchline: Using 'bros before hoes' as if it's an ironclad logical argument that applies to corpse carrying
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misapplication of bro code to an absurd situation, with everyone immediately accepting the 'logic'

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: So I'm just gonna blurt it right out. Bros before hoes.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Uh, he's right. Bros before hoes.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's got us. Oh, my God. Yeah.

jokes.text: That doesn't fit here. Bros before hoes? What does that have to do with anything? That's sort of a catch-all, Dee.
jokes.setup: Dee points out the phrase doesn't apply
jokes.punchline: Dennis treating 'bros before hoes' as a universal philosophical principle
jokes.explanation: The absurd logic that a sexist phrase is somehow universally applicable to all situations

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: That doesn't fit here. Bros before hoes? What does that have to do with anything?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: That's sort of a catch-all, Dee.

jokes.text: What if we burn the body and carry the ashes up the hill? No, no. I could consecrate them. Look, no one is going to torch or consummate all over my dad.
jokes.setup: Mac suggests burning the body and consecrating the ashes
jokes.punchline: Charlie confusing 'consecrate' with 'consummate' in defending his dad's dignity
jokes.explanation: Classic Charlie malapropism making the rejection even more crude and absurd

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: What if we burn the body and carry the ashes up the hill? No, no. I could consecrate them.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Look, no one is going to torch or consummate all over my dad.

jokes.text: Bros before hoes, Dee. Frank, can I please have a white wine spritzer? No, no, they're for Charlie. Charlie... Charlie, order me a wine spritzer, please.
jokes.setup: The group is treating 'bros before hoes' as law while carrying a corpse
jokes.punchline: This extends to needing Charlie's permission for Frank's wine spritzers
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the absurd bro code hierarchy affecting basic refreshment distribution

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Bros before hoes, Dee. Frank, can I please have a white wine spritzer?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: No, no, they're for Charlie.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Charlie... Charlie, order me a wine spritzer, please.

jokes.text: What if we chop the body up? Come on. And we carry it in pieces, right? Or-or melt it somehow. Like you did to Dee's doctor guy?
jokes.setup: Dennis suggests dismembering the body for easier transport
jokes.punchline: Frank casually references Dennis murdering Dee's doctor with hot oil
jokes.explanation: Casual reference to murder as practical precedent, showing how normalized violence has become

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What if we chop the body up? Come on. And we carry it in pieces, right? Or-or melt it somehow.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Like you did to Dee's doctor guy?

jokes.text: Well, well, that doctor showed up at the castle, and you weren't there, so Dennis poured hot oil on him. What?! Shut up! Come on!
jokes.setup: Frank reveals Dennis killed Dee's doctor
jokes.punchline: The casual way this murder is revealed mid-corpse-carrying conversation
jokes.explanation: Dark reveal of murder treated as minor inconvenience, perfect for the show's amoral universe

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Well, well, that doctor showed up at the castle, and you weren't there, so Dennis poured hot oil on him.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Shut up! Come on!

jokes.text: It-it wasn't even that hot. Did you use the murder hole?
jokes.setup: Dennis downplays the murder
jokes.punchline: Dee's excited interest in whether he used the castle's designated murder feature
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Dee's enthusiasm about murder methods overshadowing her anger about the victim

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: It-it wasn't even that hot.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Did you use the murder hole?

jokes.text: You weren't there to enjoy him so I enjoyed him, per the castle's wishes.
jokes.setup: Dennis justifying the murder to Dee
jokes.punchline: Describing murder as 'enjoying' the doctor 'per the castle's wishes'
jokes.explanation: Dennis's psychotic euphemisms and blame-shifting to an inanimate object

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: You weren't there to enjoy him so I enjoyed him, per the castle's wishes.

jokes.text: You-you killed my dad with your stupid banshee curse. My stupid what? That's why he died. Every Kelly saw a banshee right before he died. My dad saw you, so, you got to be here.
jokes.setup: Charlie blames Dee for his father's death
jokes.punchline: His logic that Dee is a banshee because she looks like one and was seen before the death
jokes.explanation: Charlie's warped Irish folklore logic connecting Dee's appearance to supernatural death omens

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You-you killed my dad with your stupid banshee curse.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: My stupid what?

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: That's why he died. Every Kelly saw a banshee right before he died. My dad saw you, so, you got to be here.

jokes.text: We don't even need you. We were probably gonna throw you from the top anyway! I thought about it. Did you think about? I did, too. God. I was thinking about it, too, yeah.
jokes.setup: The group dismisses Dee
jokes.punchline: Everyone casually admitting they planned to murder her too
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation where everyone independently considered the same murder plan

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: We don't even need you. We were probably gonna throw you from the top anyway!

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I thought about it.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Did you think about?

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I did, too.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: God.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I was thinking about it, too, yeah.

jokes.text: Well, it's that monstrous spine of hers, you know? The scoliosis hardened it into petrified wood, unlike my back which is pretty much all muscle.
jokes.setup: They realize Dee was carrying significant weight
jokes.punchline: Dennis's elaborate medical explanation for why Dee's deformed spine made her strong while his perfect back makes him weak
jokes.explanation: Dennis's vanity forcing him into absurd pseudo-science to explain functional weakness

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Well, it's that monstrous spine of hers, you know? The scoliosis hardened it into petrified wood, unlike my back which is pretty much all muscle.

jokes.text: Were you not carrying it? Not carr... What are you talking about, man? Not carrying it? Yeah, I was carrying it. Why did it get lighter? Oh, my God. It looks like he was dragging his feet back here.
jokes.setup: They notice the body feels lighter after Dennis stumbled
jokes.punchline: Discovering Dennis was hanging onto the bag instead of carrying it
jokes.explanation: Dennis's vanity leading to lazy dishonesty, caught through physical evidence

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Were you not carrying it? Not carr... What are you talking about, man? Not carrying it?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was carrying it.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Why did it get lighter?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh, my God.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: It looks like he was dragging his feet back here.

jokes.text: Yeah, I was hanging on the bag a little bit. You know, just from moment to moment, you know, just a tiny, tiny bit, okay? I was trying to decompress my Vitruvian spine. I'm sorry!
jokes.setup: Dennis is caught not actually carrying the corpse
jokes.punchline: His elaborate justification using pseudo-medical terms about his 'Vitruvian spine'
jokes.explanation: Dennis's narcissism creating elaborate excuses for basic laziness and dishonesty

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was hanging on the bag a little bit. You know, just from moment to moment, you know, just a tiny, tiny bit, okay? I was trying to decompress my Vitruvian spine. I'm sorry!

jokes.text: Now as a Dutch man, I think I would look at this the way I would think of a windmill or a dam. Cool. How's a windmill gonna help us? I haven't been Dutch long enough to know.
jokes.setup: Mac tries to apply his newfound Dutch identity to problem-solving
jokes.punchline: Immediately admitting he doesn't know anything about being Dutch
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mac moment - trying to sound authoritative about his new identity while immediately undercutting himself

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Now as a Dutch man, I think I would look at this the way I would think of a windmill or a dam. Cool. How's a windmill gonna help us? I haven't been Dutch long enough to know.

jokes.text: Oh. Oh. Okay, that's my bad. Oh. Okay. That is, uh... That's gonna be a mess inside that bag, guys. Oh, yeah. That's one big sack of Irish stew.
jokes.setup: Frank accidentally drops the corpse bag and spritzers spill inside
jokes.punchline: The corpse is now literally marinading in alcohol, described as 'Irish stew'
jokes.explanation: Dark visual comedy of corpse mixed with alcohol, with perfect food metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Oh. Oh. Okay, that's my bad. Oh. Okay. That is, uh... That's gonna be a mess inside that bag, guys. Oh, yeah. That's one big sack of Irish stew.

jokes.text: You guys are gonna want to hear this. So, uh, uh, Dutch first. Dutch first, then gay, then problem solver. Now could you repeat that back to me?
jokes.setup: Mac is reorganizing his identity priorities
jokes.punchline: Treating identity categories like a ranked list requiring confirmation from others
jokes.explanation: Mac's need to categorize and rank his identity aspects with external validation

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You guys are gonna want to hear this. So, uh, uh, Dutch first. Dutch first, then gay, then problem solver. Now could you repeat that back to me?

jokes.text: All right, you're Irish, Mac. Yeah. You're not Dutch. What? You're Irish. Right, but we knew you were gonna be so goddamn annoying about it on the trip, and that it was gonna be the only thing you talked about, so we paid your mom to tell you you were Dutch.
jokes.setup: Mac's been identifying as Dutch this whole trip
jokes.punchline: They fabricated his entire Dutch identity to avoid him being annoying about being Irish
jokes.explanation: Brilliant long-game reveal that recontextualizes Mac's entire arc - they created a fake identity to manage his annoying behavior

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: All right, you're Irish, Mac. Yeah. You're not Dutch.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: What? You're Irish.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Right, but we knew you were gonna be so goddamn annoying about it on the trip, and that it was gonna be the only thing you talked about, so we paid your mom to tell you you were Dutch.

jokes.text: It cost us a couple of loosies. Yeah. Yeah, your mom does not like you, dude.
jokes.setup: They paid Mac's mom to lie about his heritage
jokes.punchline: She did it for just a couple of cigarettes, showing how little she cares about Mac
jokes.explanation: Escalation revealing Mac's mom's indifference through the cheap price of her betrayal

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: It cost us a couple of loosies.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah, your mom does not like you, dude.

jokes.text: I'm gonna get another shamrock tattoo on the other leg! Don't you dare! Oh, I'm gonna do it! Don't you dare get another shamrock tattoo, you son of a bitch!
jokes.setup: Dennis desperately wants Mac to remove his shamrock tattoo
jokes.punchline: Mac spitefully threatens to double down with another shamrock tattoo
jokes.explanation: Mac's petty revenge through bad aesthetic choices, Dennis's horror at the prospect

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I'm gonna get another shamrock tattoo on the other leg!

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Don't you dare!

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm gonna do it!

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Don't you dare get another shamrock tattoo, you son of a bitch!

jokes.text: You know, he slaved away as a cheese monster, or whatever. He died with his lungs filled with fluid, he was gasping for air.
jokes.setup: Charlie describes his father's hard life and death
jokes.punchline: Casually describing his dad's job as 'cheese monster' without explanation
jokes.explanation: Charlie's casual use of 'cheese monster' as a job description amid emotional eulogy

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You know, he slaved away as a cheese monster, or whatever. He died with his lungs filled with fluid, he was gasping for air.

jokes.text: COVID?! But so-so the poor guy's dying from a banshee curse, and now he's got to deal with COVID on top of everything else?
jokes.setup: Frank suggests Charlie's dad died from COVID, not banshee curse
jokes.punchline: Charlie treating both as equally real medical conditions that compound each other
jokes.explanation: Charlie's worldview where supernatural curses and viruses are equally valid medical explanations

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: COVID?! But so-so the poor guy's dying from a banshee curse, and now he's got to deal with COVID on top of everything else?

jokes.text: What, do you think I'm shooting COVID laser beams out of my eyeballs?
jokes.setup: Mac suggests Dennis gave Charlie's dad COVID
jokes.punchline: Dennis's sarcastic response imagining COVID transmission as sci-fi laser beams
jokes.explanation: Dennis's dramatic flair turning COVID transmission into absurd supervillain imagery

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What, do you think I'm shooting COVID laser beams out of my eyeballs?

jokes.text: Of course there is! The-the-the hydro pills and the... and the bleach, and the... the horse dewormer. The-the president's stuff!
jokes.setup: Frank claims he got the 'non-shot' kind of vaccination
jokes.punchline: Listing dangerous COVID misinformation remedies as legitimate medical treatments
jokes.explanation: Frank's susceptibility to conspiracy theories and dangerous medical advice

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Of course there is! The-the-the hydro pills and the... and the bleach, and the... the horse dewormer. The-the president's stuff!

jokes.text: Where they put the thing in your arm with the chip, and it goes inside your body? I heard the nerd on the island talk about them. That once they get that thing inside of you, they can control your thoughts. They take your thoughts, download 'em, put 'em into a manatee.
jokes.setup: Frank explains his vaccine conspiracy theories
jokes.punchline: The bizarre escalation to downloading thoughts into manatees specifically
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of conspiracy theory into completely absurd manatee mind control

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Where they put the thing in your arm with the chip, and it goes inside your body? I heard the nerd on the island talk about them. That once they get that thing inside of you, they can control your thoughts. They take your thoughts, download 'em, put 'em into a manatee.

jokes.text: been ruined by tiny cars, castle beds and... Goddamn. I hate this godforsaken country!
jokes.setup: Dennis lists what has ruined his back
jokes.punchline: Blaming Ireland itself for his physical deterioration through infrastructure incompatible with his body
jokes.explanation: Dennis's American entitlement blaming an entire country for not accommodating his specific body

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: been ruined by tiny cars, castle beds and... Goddamn. I hate this godforsaken country!

jokes.text: For what it's worth, I'm sorry I'm not your real dad. Thanks, Frank. I'm kind of sorry, too.
jokes.setup: Frank apologizes for not being Charlie's biological father
jokes.punchline: Charlie's lukewarm response suggesting he's only 'kind of' sorry about it
jokes.explanation: Charlie's emotional detachment even in touching moments, the understated response

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: For what it's worth, I'm sorry I'm not your real dad.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Thanks, Frank. I'm kind of sorry, too.

jokes.text: Aah! Whoa! Is that piss? Oh! That's not supposed to be the piss one! Why do you have a piss one?
jokes.setup: Frank offers Charlie Gatorade as comfort
jokes.punchline: It's actually piss from the canteen mix-up earlier
jokes.explanation: Payoff to earlier piss canteen setup, ruining the touching moment with Frank's grossness

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Aah! Whoa! Is that piss? Oh! That's not supposed to be the piss one! Why do you have a piss one?

jokes.text: Oh, give me that. Wash it out with Gatorade. Goddamn it, Frank! Ooh. Huh? That's piss, too! Oh, no!
jokes.setup: Frank tries to fix the piss problem with the other canteen
jokes.punchline: The backup is also piss, meaning Frank has no actual beverages
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - Frank's confusion means Charlie gets double piss instead of relief

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh, give me that. Wash it out with Gatorade.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Goddamn it, Frank!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Ooh.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Huh?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: That's piss, too!

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh, no!

jokes.text: Oh, I must have got confused and pissed in 'em both. Wait, wait, wait. I got you a spritzer! How about a spritzer?
jokes.setup: Frank realizes both canteens contain piss
jokes.punchline: Immediately offering wine spritzers as if alcohol is the logical solution to piss consumption
jokes.explanation: Frank's obliviousness to appropriate solutions, treating alcohol as universal remedy

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Oh, I must have got confused and pissed in 'em both. Wait, wait, wait. I got you a spritzer! How about a spritzer?

jokes.text: Oh, Dee, don't worry about it, because as soon as I figure out who or what I am, you'll never see me again! You're Irish. You're Irish. You are Irish! You are Irish! You're an Irishman! Shh!
jokes.setup: Mac threatens to disappear once he finds his identity
jokes.punchline: Everyone desperately shouting his obvious Irish identity while he shushes them
jokes.explanation: Mac's willful denial while everyone states the obvious truth, building to absurd crescendo

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Oh, Dee, don't worry about it, because as soon as I figure out who or what I am, you'll never see me again!

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: You're Irish.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You're Irish.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: You are Irish!

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: You are Irish!

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: You're an Irishman!

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Shh!

jokes.text: I will speak at whatever volume I choose, and if you don't like it, you can suck my big fat dick! One volume? For every single person in the whole wide world? That's socialism, and I won't stand for it!
jokes.setup: Irish person asks Americans to speak more quietly
jokes.punchline: Dee treating volume regulation as socialist oppression, escalating to political ideology
jokes.explanation: Perfect ugly American moment - turning basic courtesy into political oppression with vulgar defiance

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I will speak at whatever volume I choose, and if you don't like it, you can suck my big fat dick! One volume? For every single person in the whole wide world? That's socialism, and I won't stand for it!

jokes.text: We are America, sweetheart! And we carry our country with us wherever we go! Because we love her! And when you love someone, you can't bear to leave 'em behind! Not ever!
jokes.setup: Irish person points out they're not in America
jokes.punchline: Dee's jingoistic speech about carrying America everywhere, ironically delivered while carrying a corpse
jokes.explanation: Ironic patriotic speech delivered while literally carrying a dead body - perfect thematic metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: We are America, sweetheart! And we carry our country with us wherever we go! Because we love her! And when you love someone, you can't bear to leave 'em behind! Not ever!

jokes.text: This isn't fair. I shouldn't have to carry you up this hill. You never carried me up a hill. You never picked me up from school. You didn't read me bedtime stories. You didn't carry me on your shoulders. You didn't bounce me on... You weren't there!
jokes.setup: Charlie alone with his dead father's corpse
jokes.punchline: The emotional breakdown revealing his daddy issues through literal carrying metaphors
jokes.explanation: Brilliant emotional core connecting physical carrying to emotional abandonment - Charlie's deepest pain

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: This isn't fair. I shouldn't have to carry you up this hill. You never carried me up a hill. You never picked me up from school. You didn't read me bedtime stories. You didn't carry me on your shoulders. You didn't bounce me on... You weren't there!

jokes.text: Ah, Frank's right. I'm glad you're dead. I'm glad you're dead. Now I don't have to spend the rest of my life waiting for you to pick me up.
jokes.setup: Charlie's emotional breakdown continues
jokes.punchline: Finding relief in his father's death because it ends his hope for paternal connection
jokes.explanation: Devastating dark comedy - death as relief from endless disappointment, perfectly captures Charlie's damaged psychology

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Ah, Frank's right. I'm glad you're dead. I'm glad you're dead. Now I don't have to spend the rest of my life waiting for you to pick me up.

jokes.text: It was supposed to go in the water. Maybe they usually do this at high tide. They probably did, yeah. Yeah. Well, the birds will get him.
jokes.setup: They throw the corpse off the cliff but it doesn't reach the water
jokes.punchline: Casually accepting that birds will dispose of the body as plan B
jokes.explanation: Dark casualness about corpse disposal failure, treating scavengers as adequate funeral plan

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: It was supposed to go in the water. Maybe they usually do this at high tide. They probably did, yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah. Well, the birds will get him.

jokes.text: But there's a bunch of kids down there. Oh, there's kids! Are there kids? Oh, shit. Yeah. Oh, that's good, then. They'll find the body. And they'll call the cops. It-It'll be sort of like a Stand by Me situation.
jokes.setup: They realize children are near where the corpse landed
jokes.punchline: Comparing traumatizing children with a corpse to the nostalgic movie Stand by Me
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy of treating child trauma as heartwarming coming-of-age experience via movie reference

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: But there's a bunch of kids down there.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh, there's kids! Are there kids?

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Oh, shit. Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh, that's good, then. They'll find the body. And they'll call the cops. It-It'll be sort of like a Stand by Me situation.

jokes.text: You want to get some stew? God, no. That stew's gross. Yeah, man. Stew? Oh, it is kind of nasty. Yeah. You guys want to go to McDonald's? Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Oh. Oh, McDonald's. USA! USA! USA!
jokes.setup: After disposing of the corpse, they consider food options
jokes.punchline: Rejecting Irish stew for McDonald's and breaking into patriotic chant
jokes.explanation: Ugly American ending - choosing corporate food over local cuisine, followed by jingoistic celebration

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You want to get some stew?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: God, no. That stew's gross.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Yeah, man. Stew?

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Oh, it is kind of nasty.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You guys want to go to McDonald's?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Oh.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Oh, McDonald's.
USA! USA! USA!

tmdb_overview: The Gang attempts to ascend a mountain in order for Charlie to fulfill an old and mysterious Irish burial tradition. One by one, the Gang backs out until Charlie is left to honor the dead body alone.
